title: Sectoral Technology and Structural Transformation
abstract: We assess how the properties of technology affect structural transformation, i.e., the reallocation of production factors across the broad sectors of agriculture, manufacturing, and services. To this end, we estimate sectoral constant elasticity of substitution (CES) and Cobb-Douglas production functions on postwar US data. We find that differences in technical progress across the three sectors are the dominant force behind structural transformation whereas other differences across sectoral technology are of second-order importance. Our findings imply that Cobb-Douglas sectoral production functions that differ only in technical progress capture the main technological forces behind the postwar US structural transformation. (JEL E16, E25, O33, O47)
summary_en: Why do economies reallocate labour out of agriculture, then manufacturing, and into services as they develop? This paper fits sectoral production functions to postwar US data and finds that differences in technical progress across sectors are the dominant driver of structural transformation — not differences in capital intensity, substitution elasticities, or input requirements. The implication: a simple model with Cobb–Douglas sectoral production functions and sector-specific technical progress captures most of the transformation dynamics. Useful for anyone calibrating multi-sector growth models for developing or transition economies.
data_used: US postwar sectoral data, 1947–2010: value added and factor inputs for agriculture, manufacturing, and services from the BEA and BLS; supplemented with input-output tables.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian macro-diagnostic and long-run-growth discussions: the paper supports a view of structural change as technology-driven, which implies Hungarian deindustrialisation since the 1990s is largely natural, not a policy failure. Relevant framing for Ministry of Economy debates on "reindustrialisation" policies — such policies face long-run technology headwinds. Uses US data; Hungarian technology paths differ but the conceptual framework transfers.
